root_text.sn52.8:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā anuruddho sāvatthiyaṁ viharati salaḷāgāre.
root_text.sn52.8:1.2: Tatra kho āyasmā anuruddho bhikkhū āmantesi …pe… etadavoca:
root_text.sn52.8:1.3: “seyyathāpi, āvuso, gaṅgā nadī pācīnaninnā pācīnapoṇā pācīnapabbhārā.
root_text.sn52.8:1.5: ‘mayaṁ imaṁ gaṅgānadiṁ pacchāninnaṁ karissāma pacchāpoṇaṁ pacchāpabbhāran’ti.
root_text.sn52.8:1.6: Taṁ kiṁ maññathāvuso, api nu so mahājanakāyo gaṅgānadiṁ pacchāninnaṁ kareyya pacchāpoṇaṁ pacchāpabbhāran”ti?
root_text.sn52.8:1.10: Sā na sukarā pacchāninnaṁ kātuṁ pacchāpoṇaṁ pacchāpabbhāraṁ.
root_text.sn52.8:1.11: Yāvadeva ca pana so mahājanakāyo kilamathassa vighātassa bhāgī assā”ti.
root_text.sn52.8:2.1: “Evameva kho, āvuso, bhikkhuṁ cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bhāventaṁ cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bahulīkarontaṁ rājāno vā rājamahāmattā vā mittā vā amaccā vā ñātī vā sālohitā vā bhogehi abhihaṭṭhuṁ pavāreyyuṁ:
root_text.sn52.8:2.2: ‘ehambho purisa, kiṁ te ime kāsāvā anudahanti?
root_text.sn52.8:2.4: Ehi hīnāyāvattitvā bhoge ca bhuñjassu puññāni ca karohī’ti.
root_text.sn52.8:3.1: So vata, āvuso, bhikkhu cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bhāvento cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bahulīkaronto sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvattissatīti—netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn52.8:3.3: Yañhi taṁ, āvuso, cittaṁ dīgharattaṁ vivekaninnaṁ vivekapoṇaṁ vivekapabbhāraṁ taṁ vata hīnāyāvattissatīti—netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn52.8:3.4: Kathañcāvuso, bhikkhu cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bhāveti, cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bahulīkarotīti?
root_text.sn52.8:3.8: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn52.8:3.9: Evaṁ kho, āvuso, bhikkhu cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bhāveti, cattāro satipaṭṭhāne bahulīkarotī”ti.

translation_text.sn52.8:1.1: At one time Venerable Anuruddha was staying near Sāvatthī in the frankincense-tree hut.
translation_text.sn52.8:1.2: There Venerable Anuruddha addressed the mendicants:
translation_text.sn52.8:1.3: “Reverends, suppose that, although the Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east,
translation_text.sn52.8:1.4: a large crowd were to come along with a spade and basket, saying:
translation_text.sn52.8:1.5: ‘We’ll make this Ganges river slant, slope, and incline to the west!’
translation_text.sn52.8:1.6: What do you think, reverends? Would they succeed?”
translation_text.sn52.8:1.9: The Ganges river slants, slopes, and inclines to the east.
translation_text.sn52.8:1.10: It’s not easy to make it slant, slope, and incline to the west.
translation_text.sn52.8:1.11: That large crowd will eventually get weary and frustrated.”
translation_text.sn52.8:2.1: “In the same way, while a mendicant develops and cultivates the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, if rulers or their chief ministers, friends or colleagues, relatives or family should invite them to accept wealth, saying:
translation_text.sn52.8:2.2: ‘Please, my friend, why let these ocher robes torment you?
translation_text.sn52.8:2.3: Why follow the practice of shaving your head and carrying an alms bowl?
translation_text.sn52.8:2.4: Come, return to a lesser life, enjoy wealth, and make merit!’
translation_text.sn52.8:3.1: It is quite impossible for a mendicant who is developing and cultivating the four kinds of mindfulness meditation to resign the training and return to a lesser life.
translation_text.sn52.8:3.3: Because for a long time that mendicant’s mind has slanted, sloped, and inclined to seclusion. So it’s impossible for them to return to a lesser life.
translation_text.sn52.8:3.4: And how does a mendicant develop and cultivate the four kinds of mindfulness meditation?
translation_text.sn52.8:3.5: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body …
translation_text.sn52.8:3.8: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn52.8:3.9: That’s how a mendicant develops and cultivates the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.”
